items.title: DPRK-Related Campaigns with LNK and GitHub C2
items.description: FortiGuard Labs recently detected a series of LNK files targeting users in South Korea. These attacks use a multi-stage scripting process and leverage GitHub as Command and Control (C2) infrastructure to evade detection. Although these LNK files can be traced back to 2024, earlier versions had less obfuscation and contained significant metadata, allowing us to track similar attacks spreading the X
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a multi-stage scripting process using LNK files to target users in South Korea, leveraging GitHub as Command and Control infrastructure to evade detection. The attacks utilize various TTPs, including web services and obfuscated files, to impair defenses and establish multi-stage channels. To defend against this threat, it is essential to implement robust email and web filtering to block suspicious LNK files and GitHub traffic.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1583.006| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Iranian APT Infrastructure in Focus: Mapping State-Aligned Clusters During Geopolitical Escalation
items.description: The analysis examines Iranian state-aligned threat actors and their infrastructure patterns during heightened geopolitical tensions. It focuses on mapping network infrastructure, ASN patterns, TLS fingerprints, and hosting clusters associated with various Iranian APT groups. The report highlights the importance of proactive infrastructure monitoring to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations
items.ai_summary: Iranian state-aligned threat actors are utilizing various tactics to gather victim host information and steal or forge authentication certificates, posing a significant threat to organizations. The threat actors' infrastructure patterns, including network infrastructure and hosting clusters, can be monitored to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations. To defend against these threats, organizations should prioritize proactive infrastructure monitoring and implement robust authentication mechanisms.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Discovery]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: MuddyWater Exposed: Inside an Iranian APT operation
items.description: Researchers identified and analyzed exposed infrastructure of MuddyWater, an Iranian cyber espionage group linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security. The investigation revealed their reconnaissance methods, exploitation of vulnerabilities, custom command and control frameworks, and exfiltration techniques. Targets included organizations in Israel, Jordan, Egypt, UAE, Portugal, and the US
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian APT group, conducts cyber espionage by exploiting vulnerabilities and using custom command and control frameworks to exfiltrate data from targeted organizations. The group's reconnaissance methods and exfiltration techniques pose a significant threat to organizations in various sectors. To defend against MuddyWater, organizations should prioritize implementing robust network monitoring and intrusion detection systems to identify and block suspicious C2 communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: South American telecommunication providers targeted with three new malware implants
items.description: UAT-9244, a China-nexus advanced persistent threat actor, has been targeting critical telecommunications infrastructure in South America since 2024. The group employs three new malware implants: TernDoor, a Windows-based backdoor variant of CrowDoor; PeerTime, an ELF-based backdoor using BitTorrent protocol; and BruteEntry, a brute force scanner for SSH, Postgres, and Tomcat servers. UAT-9244 uses
items.ai_summary: UAT-9244, a China-nexus APT, targets South American telecoms with three new malware implants: TernDoor, PeerTime, and BruteEntry. These implants enable backdoor access, brute force attacks, and remote access. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1190, T1110, and T1219 TTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110| C[Brute Force]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An Investigation Into Years of Undetected Operations Targeting High-Value Sectors
items.description: Since 2020, a Chinese threat actor dubbed CL-UNK-1068 has been targeting high-value organizations across South, Southeast and East Asia, focusing on critical sectors like aviation, energy, government, and telecommunications. The group employs a diverse toolkit including custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries to maintain stealthy persistence. Their techniqu
items.ai_summary: CL-UNK-1068, a Chinese threat actor, has been undetected since 2020, targeting high-value sectors in Asia with custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries. Their techniques include exploiting public-facing applications and developing capabilities. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1587 and T1190 to detect potential CL-UNK-1068 activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1587| B[Develop Capabilities]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Middle East Conflict Fuels Opportunistic Cyber Attacks
items.description: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has triggered a surge in cybercriminal activity. Over 8,000 newly registered domains with conflict-related keywords have been identified, many of which may be weaponized in future campaigns. Multiple cases of malicious activity have been observed, including targeted attacks using conflict-themed lures, deployment of the LOTUSLITE backdoor, fake news blogs le
items.ai_summary: The ongoing Middle East conflict has triggered a surge in cybercriminal activity, with over 8,000 newly registered domains potentially weaponized for future campaigns. Threat actors are using conflict-themed lures and deploying the LOTUSLITE backdoor in targeted attacks. Defenders should monitor for suspicious domains and user execution tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic

items.title: Unmasking an Attack Chain of MuddyWater
items.description: An intrusion attributed to MuddyWater, an Iranian-linked APT, was identified in a customer environment. The attack involved initial access through RDP, establishing an SSH tunnel, and deploying malware via DLL side-loading. The threat actor used FMAPP.exe, a legitimate Fortemedia Inc. application, to load a malicious FMAPP.dll for C2 communications. The timeline of activities revealed typos in com
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian-linked APT, exploited RDP for initial access, established an SSH tunnel, and deployed malware via DLL side-loading. The threat actor used FMAPP.exe to load a malicious FMAPP.dll for C2 communications. Defenders should prioritize monitoring RDP access and DLL loading activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1574.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Seqrite: Advisory: Middle East Conflict & Cyber Escalation
items.description: Iran’s cyber ecosystem operates through a layered structure that combines state-directed APT groups (IRGC-linked and MOIS-linked); semi-official contractors and front entities; hacktivist personas and collectives operated by intelligence services; and ideologically aligned foreign collectives operating in parallel. The Stryker Corporation attack on March 11, 2026 marked a significant escalation: a
items.ai_summary: Iran's cyber ecosystem, including state-directed APT groups and hacktivist personas, has escalated cyber attacks, as seen in the Stryker Corporation attack. This escalation involves stealing or forging authentication certificates and defacement. Defenders should prioritize monitoring authentication certificate integrity and network access controls.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic

items.title: Inside an AIenabled device code phishing campaign
items.description: Microsoft Defender Security Research has observed a widespread phishing campaign leveraging the Device Code Authentication flow to compromise organizational accounts at scale. While traditional device code attacks are typically narrow in scope, this campaign demonstrated a higher success rate, driven by automation and dynamic code generation that circumvented the standard 15-minute expiration wind
items.ai_summary: A widespread phishing campaign uses AI-enabled device code authentication to compromise organizational accounts at scale, leveraging automation and dynamic code generation to evade traditional security measures. This campaign demonstrates a higher success rate compared to traditional device code attacks. Defenders should prioritize monitoring and blocking suspicious device code authentication requests.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: Storm-1175 focuses gaze on vulnerable web-facing assets in high ...
items.description: The financially motivated cybercriminal actor tracked by Microsoft Threat Intelligence as Storm-1175 operates high-velocity ransomware campaigns that weaponize N-days, targeting vulnerable, web-facing systems during the window between vulnerability disclosure and widespread patch adoption. Following successful exploitation, Storm-1175 rapidly moves from initial access to data exfiltration and depl
items.ai_summary: Storm-1175 is a financially motivated cybercriminal actor that targets vulnerable web-facing systems using N-days, leading to high-velocity ransomware campaigns. They rapidly move from initial access to data exfiltration and encryption. Defenders should prioritize patching vulnerable systems to prevent initial access.

items.title: CVE-2026-4994: A vulnerability was found in wandb OpenUI up to 1.0/3.5-turb. Affected is the fu...
items.description: A vulnerability was found in wandb OpenUI up to 1.0/3.5-turb. Affected is the function generic_exception_handler of the file backend/openui/server.py of the component APIStatusError Handler. The manipulation of the argument key results in information exposure through error message. Access to the local network is required for this attack. The exploit has been made public and could be used. The vend
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4994 is a vulnerability in wandb OpenUI that allows information exposure through error messages. An attacker requires local network access to exploit this vulnerability. Defenders should update wandb OpenUI to a non-affected version.

items.description: A vulnerability has been found in wandb OpenUI up to 0.0.0.0/1.0. This impacts an unknown function of the file backend/openui/config.py. The manipulation of the argument LITELLM_MASTER_KEY leads to hard-coded credentials. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4993 is a vulnerability in wandb OpenUI up to 0.0.0.0/1.0, allowing local exploitation via hard-coded credentials. This impacts an unknown function in the file backend/openui/config.py. Defenders should update to the latest version to mitigate this vulnerability.

This is due to the contact form handler performing placeholder substitution on attacker-controlled form fields and then passing the resulting values into email headers without removing CR/LF chara
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-2442 is a CRLF Injection vulnerability in the Pagelayer WordPress plugin, allowing attackers to inject malicious email headers. This vulnerability affects all versions up to 2.0.7. To defend, update the plugin to a patched version.

items.title:
CVE-2026-23399: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

nf_tables: ...
items.description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

nf_tables: nft_dynset: fix possible stateful expression memleak in error path

If cloning the second stateful expression in the element via GFP_ATOMIC
fails, then the first stateful expression remains in place without being
released.

   unreferenced object (percpu) 0x607b97e9cab8 (size 16):
     comm "softirq", pid 0, jiffies 42
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-23399 is a Linux kernel vulnerability that allows for a memory leak in the nf_tables module. This vulnerability is not exploitable, but it can lead to a denial-of-service condition. To defend against this vulnerability, ensure that Linux kernel updates are applied promptly.

graph LR

    C -->|None| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.description: The Oxygen Theme theme for WordPress is vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery in all versions up to, and including, 6.0.8 via the laborator_calc_route AJAX action. This makes it possible for unauthenticated attackers to make web requests to arbitrary locations originating from the web application and can be used to query and modify information from internal services.

items.description: The eswifi socket offload driver copies user-provided payloads into a fixed buffer without checking available space; oversized sends overflow `eswifi->buf`, corrupting kernel memory (CWE-120). items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Axios Hack, Chrome 0-Day, Fortinet Exploits, Paragon Spyware and More
items.description: This week had real hits. The key software got tampered with. Active bugs showed up in the tools people use every day. Some attacks didn’t even need much effort because the path was already there. One weak spot now spreads wider than before. What starts small can reach a lot of systems fast. items.description: The most active piece of enterprise infrastructure in the company is the developer workstation. items.title: BKA Identifies REvil Leaders Behind 130 German Ransomware Attacks
items.description: Germany's Federal Criminal Police Office (aka BKA or the Bundeskriminalamt) has unmasked the real identities of two of the key figures associated with the now-defunct REvil (aka Sodinokibi) ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation. items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.description: In case of a cyber incident, most organizations fear more of data loss (via exfiltration) than regular data encryption because they have a good backup policy in place. items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.
items.workflow_graph:

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
